title: Repo-wide refactor
description: A long-context agent pass over a medium repository: broad file reads, patch synthesis, test output and a final review.

events.text: Refactor the race experience end to end. Read the product brief, current route, shared simulator components, picker helpers, tests and styling before touching code. Keep the implementation tight and verify the browser flow.

Repository context loaded: RacePage owns URL state and dual playback; SimulatorPieces contains SearchSelect, phase UI and lane rendering; configMatrix has top-down result resolution; styles define command panel, picker strip, lanes and mobile breakpoints. Existing scenarios are short and do not stress context depth.

cacheBust.reason: Large multi-file read inserted into the working transcript.

events.text:
Okay, everything is loaded now, so let me actually work through what "refactor the race experience end to end" means before I touch a single line. The read confirms what I suspected from the file list: RacePage.tsx is the owner of both URL state and dual playback, which means it is carrying two separate concerns that should probably stay separate at the module boundary even if the page component still wires them together at the top. SimulatorPieces.tsx is the shared UI kit — SearchSelect, phase indicators, lane rendering — and configMatrix.ts already does top-down result resolution, so there is a foundation here, not a blank page. The styles file defines a command panel, a picker strip, the lane layout, and mobile breakpoints, which tells me the current page is organized around a persistent top strip of controls sitting above two lanes. And the note that existing scenarios are short and don't stress context depth is the other half of this task — the brief explicitly says "verify the browser flow" and "keep the implementation tight," so whatever I build has to hold up all the way through a long agent-style transcript, not just the short chat and short refactor scripts that exist today.

Let me start with the part of "feel" that's easiest to get wrong: start and stop. Right now, once a race has been run once, the natural pattern in a lot of UIs like this is to relabel the primary button "Restart" — the assumption being that starting again is the only thing a user would want. But think about what that actually implies. A user hits Start, watches both lanes stream for a few seconds, decides they picked the wrong hardware for lane B, and now wants to stop and reconfigure. If the only affordance is "Restart," they have two bad options: let it keep playing while they change lane B underneath a running race, or hit Restart and watch it immediately start streaming again from zero with the old, wrong configuration, then have to stop that too. Neither of those is "clean start/stop semantics." What the interaction actually needs is a strict two-state toggle: Start when the race hasn't been engaged, Stop when it's running or paused mid-run, and Stop always means "go back to the ready state," not "go back to the ready state and then immediately re-engage." That maps directly onto a race clock that counts up while active and freezes once a lane finishes — if Stop doesn't zero both lanes' elapsed time and playback position, the clock and the lanes fall out of sync, and the whole "central clock" framing stops meaning anything. So: the run button needs to expose exactly two intents, Start and Stop, and Stop needs to be a hard reset of both playback instances, not a semantic alias for restart. That also simplifies the button component itself — no third "paused" state to design for, no ambiguous middle ground, just running or not running, which is easier to reason about and easier to keep the two lanes honest against.

Now, match suggestions — this is the part of the ask that's doing the most product work, because "keep the implementation tight" and "the browser flow" both hinge on the user not getting lost in the picker grid. If someone picks a strong result for lane A, what should lane B default to, and what should the suggestions panel actually rank? The obvious naive approach is "just show everything and let the user filter," but that's the current state and it's presumably part of what's being asked to change — otherwise there's no reason to read raceComparison-adjacent groundwork or think about ranking at all here. So the suggestion logic needs weights, not a boolean same-model filter. Same model should dominate — if two rows share a model id, that is by far the strongest signal that a race between them is actually informative, because everything downstream (context handling, tokenizer, output style) is held constant. Hardware differing is actually a point in favor for a suggestion, not against, when the model is already fixed, because the entire premise of "compare hardware" needs the hardware to vary — if the model is fixed and the hardware is also fixed there's nothing left to race except noise. But if the model isn't shared at all, falling back to "same model family" gives a weaker but still defensible suggestion — related architectures tend to have comparable behavior even if the raw numbers move. Quant should count for something too, since matching quant removes one more confound, and runtime — name and backend both, not just an opaque key — because "llama.cpp on Metal" and "llama.cpp on CUDA" are close enough that a user probably still wants to see them suggested even without an exact runtime match. And depth: candidates that only have shallow measurements are just less interesting to race, because the streaming feel is more informative when there's more context loaded, so depth should nudge the score without dominating it — something like a log-scaled bonus so an extra doubling of measured depth gives a diminishing return instead of letting one very deep result crowd out everything else. Ties should break deterministically, by id, so re-renders don't jitter, and there should be a minimum score threshold below which a "suggestion" isn't actually a suggestion — showing three barely-related rows just because a slot needs filling is worse than showing fewer, stronger ones, or none at all if nothing clears the bar. I keep going back and forth on whether hardware match should also add a small bonus for "this is a machine I'd actually want a race between" versus penalizing exact-hardware duplicates, but the case for penalizing it is stronger: identical hardware plus identical model is not really a race, it should barely differ except runtime, and if that's the whole story it belongs one tier below "hardware is the main variable," not above it.

That leads straight into the harder architectural question: where does this ranking logic live? The lazy path is to write it inline in RacePage.tsx, right next to the JSX that consumes it, since that's already where the config-selection wiring sits. I can feel the pull of that option because it would be the smallest diff and the fastest way to get something on screen. But it's wrong for a few concrete reasons, not just a vague "separate concerns" instinct. First, this scoring function has real edge cases — ties, thresholds, the depth bonus curve, family fallback versus no fallback — and the only sane way to pin those down is with unit tests that assert exact rankings for constructed fixtures, and you cannot unit test something that's a closure defined inside a component render without mounting the component and reaching into its internals, which is slow and brittle and tests React rendering as much as it tests the ranking. Second, RacePage already owns url-sync effects, two playback hooks, and share-link construction; piling ranking math into the same file makes the file harder to scan and makes it more likely that a future change to the ranking logic accidentally touches rendering, or vice versa. Third — and this is the part that actually settles it — the "helper logic should be pure" framing only works if the logic genuinely has no dependency on anything React-specific: no hooks, no refs, just catalog data in, ranked suggestions out. That's a function signature I can write down right now: something like scoring a candidate result against an anchor result and the catalog, returning a score and a short human-readable reason string ("same model, quant and runtime" versus "exploratory comparison"), and a separate function that filters the whole catalog through that scorer and returns the top N. If I can write that signature without importing anything from React, it belongs in src/lib next to configMatrix.ts, not inside the page component. So: a small dedicated module, colocated test file, pure functions, and RacePage imports from it instead of owning the math.

While I'm at it, the same argument applies to a second piece of logic that's currently tangled into the picker interaction: resolving what changes when a user edits one field of a lane's configuration. If someone flips lane A's runtime, and the current model/hardware/quant combination has no result for that runtime, something has to pick the closest remaining match instead of just erroring or resetting to some arbitrary default. That's a preservation-scoring problem, not a picker-rendering problem — model match should count more than hardware match, hardware more than runtime, runtime more than quant, roughly mirroring how disruptive it is to lose each one, and ties should again break by id for determinism. That belongs in the same helper module, exported alongside the suggestion ranking, because it's the same category of pure "score candidates, pick a winner" logic and testing it the same way — fixed fixtures, asserted output — is far more reliable than clicking through the UI and eyeballing whether the right option got selected.

There's a related decision about how strong a "comparison" actually is, separate from ranking suggestions — once two results are chosen for lane A and lane B, the page needs to say something honest about what kind of comparison this is: same model/quant/runtime with hardware as the only variable is the strongest, cleanest story; same model with hardware and something else varying is still useful but noisier; different models entirely is explicitly exploratory and should be labeled as such rather than implied to be a fair fight. That's also naturally a pure function of two results and the catalog — no reason to compute it inline either.

Now the layout question, which is really a question about what the top of the page is for. A command strip mixing a run button, speed control, cache mode toggle, and a share action alongside separate lane pickers scattered elsewhere is asking a first-time visitor to understand five different controls before they understand the one thing that matters: what is lane A and what is lane B. The fix is to stop treating "controls" and "setup" as separate zones and build one workbench: a compact header carrying the comparison label and the run/share/speed/cache controls together, since those genuinely are commands, and a builder row underneath with a setup card for lane A, a scenario card in the middle, and a setup card for lane B. Each setup card should let the user start from whichever axis matters most to them — model first, hardware first, or runtime first — and cascade the remaining fields as constrained searches once the first one is picked, so users aren't hunting through unconstrained lists. Putting the suggestions panel inside the scenario card, labeled clearly as suggestions for the other lane, keeps it discoverable without forcing it — a suggestion is a one-click shortcut, not a requirement, and someone who wants an intentionally mismatched, exploratory comparison should still be able to reach it through the ordinary pickers.

Wait — before I move on, let me reconsider the threshold question, because I glossed over it. "Minimum score below which a suggestion isn't shown" is easy to say and hard to actually pin a number to without thinking about what happens at the boundary. If the cutoff is too low, users see three or four barely-related rows padded out to fill a fixed-size panel, which is worse than showing one strong suggestion and leaving the rest of the space empty — a sparse but honest panel beats a full but misleading one. If the cutoff is too high, users with a genuinely unusual anchor result — some rarely-benchmarked hardware and model pairing — get no suggestions at all, which reads as broken rather than "nothing good enough exists yet." I think the right instinct is to bias toward the second failure mode rather than the first: an empty suggestions panel is at least honest about what it's telling the user, whereas a panel full of borderline rows actively teaches the wrong lesson about what a fair comparison looks like. So the panel should be allowed to render with fewer than the target number of suggestions, or none, rather than backfilling with anything that technically passed a lower bar. That also means the panel copy needs to read fine at zero results, not just assume there's always something to show — small thing, but it's the kind of detail that turns into a visible empty box in the browser pass if I don't think about it now.

There's also a naming question I keep circling back to, and it matters more than it should because whatever name I pick now is what every future contributor will grep for. "Comparison" logic living in a module about the race feature specifically — not shared with Configs or Playground — argues for a name scoped to race, not a generic "comparison" module that could be mistaken for something catalog-wide. Something like a race-comparison module reads clearly next to a race-session module handling the setup-reset question (does changing a field mid-run need to clear playback state) and a race-output module handling the sliding window of visible transcript events per lane. Splitting those three apart instead of dumping everything into one large race-helpers file keeps each one small enough to read in one sitting, and keeps the pure-ranking math physically separate from the "does this change invalidate the current run" question, which is a genuinely different kind of logic even though both are triggered by the same picker interactions.

One more thing worth reasoning through now rather than discovering it in the browser: the builder row of three cards — lane A setup, scenario picker, lane B setup — needs to survive the mobile breakpoint the styles file already defines for the lane layout. Three cards side by side is fine on a wide viewport, but forcing that same three-column arrangement down to phone width is exactly the kind of thing that produces horizontal overflow, and "the implementation tight" plus "verify the browser flow" both imply this has to actually be checked, not assumed. The safer default is to let the grid collapse to a single column under the same breakpoint the lanes already use, so lane A's card, the scenario card, and lane B's card stack vertically with the run controls staying reachable above the fold. That's a CSS decision more than a logic decision, but it belongs in the plan now because it constrains how the setup card component gets built — it can't assume a fixed multi-column context, its internal field grid needs to reflow on its own regardless of how many columns the outer builder row currently has.

Last piece: the long-context scenario. Every existing scenario is short enough that it never really exercises what a long agent session actually feels like — deep tool output, thinking interleaved with tool calls, a context window that has to survive a cache-busting event or two along the way. If race timing is supposed to reflect real agent work, and this refactor is explicitly about making the race experience trustworthy under realistic load, then the missing case is exactly that: a scenario that reads several files, reasons about a nontrivial plan, applies a patch, reasons again about verification, and reports back — the kind of session where context depth actually crosses into territory current scenarios don't reach, and where the streaming feel of thinking versus tool wait versus decode actually has room to differ meaningfully between two lanes. It has to stay readable under the new layout too — long generated text streaming into a fixed-height output panel needs the same scroll-to-active behavior the lane already has for shorter events, just exercised harder.

Putting all of that together: split the ranking and selection-resolution logic out of RacePage into a small, pure, colocated-tested helper module; replace the top command strip with a compare workbench that puts lane setup, scenario choice, and suggestions in one builder row under a compact command header; collapse the run button down to a strict Start/Stop toggle where Stop always resets both lanes to zero instead of aliasing to restart; and add a long-context, multi-turn agent scenario so the whole thing gets exercised under conditions that actually resemble a real session instead of only short scripted exchanges. That's the plan, and it's specific enough to start writing.

I will split comparison ranking into a small helper module, replace the top command strip with a compare workbench, make running state expose Stop instead of Restart, and add a long-context scenario so race timing reflects real agent work. Here is each piece in more detail.

First, the helper module. Comparison ranking, selection resolution, and comparison labeling all currently want to live somewhere with no dependency on React, so they can be tested with plain fixtures instead of mounted components. I will pull them into a small module scoped to the race feature rather than a generic catalog-wide comparison file, since this logic is specific to how race lanes get set up and compared, not something Configs or Playground need. The suggestion function will take an anchor result, the catalog, and a limit, and return a ranked list of candidates, each carrying the candidate result itself, a numeric score, and a short human-readable reason string — "same model, quant and runtime," "same model family," "exploratory comparison" — so the UI never has to reconstruct why something was suggested; it just displays what the scoring already decided. The scoring itself weighs same-model matches heaviest, treats a hardware difference as a positive signal once the model is fixed (since hardware is supposed to be the variable in the default comparison), gives partial credit for shared family when the model differs, adds smaller bonuses for matching quant and runtime, and applies a log-scaled bonus for measured context depth so deeper, more informative results edge out shallow ones without one very deep row dominating everything. Ties break deterministically by result id, and anything that doesn't clear a minimum score is dropped rather than padded in — an empty suggestions panel is more honest than a full one made of weak matches.

Next to that, a selection-resolution function handles the case where changing one field of a lane's setup — say, switching lane A to a different runtime — leaves no result matching the rest of the current selection. Instead of erroring or resetting to an arbitrary default, it re-ranks the remaining candidates by how much of the current selection each one preserves: matching model counts for the most, then hardware, then runtime, then quant, mirroring how disruptive losing each one actually is to the comparison the user was building. A third function turns a chosen pair of results into a comparison label and a short explanation — "Hardware comparison: same model, quant and runtime, the hardware is the main variable" down through "Exploratory comparison: different models, use this deliberately" — so the page can state plainly what kind of race the user has actually set up instead of leaving them to infer it from the pickers. And the race verdict itself will compare each lane's measured wall-time range rather than a single point estimate, so if the two lanes' plausible ranges overlap, the result is reported as too close to call instead of crowning a winner from noise. Two adjacent, equally small modules round this out: one holding the "does this setup change invalidate the current run" check that clears both lanes' playback state when a field changes mid-race or mid-completion, and one holding the sliding window of transcript events a lane keeps visible while streaming, so long transcripts don't try to render every event that has already scrolled past. Each of these ships with a colocated test file asserting exact rankings and resolutions against constructed fixtures, so the ranking behavior is pinned down independent of anything rendering-related. The field-order question that drives the cascading pickers — which axis gets asked first when a user starts from model versus hardware versus runtime — will also live in this module as a small lookup keyed by mode, so the setup card component only has to read an order and render fields in that sequence rather than encoding the branching itself; that keeps the three orders (model-first, hardware-first, runtime-first) declared in one place instead of scattered across conditional JSX.

I also want the comparison-label function to expose a coarse "how strong is this comparison" level alongside its text, not just the sentence itself, because the workbench header will use that level to choose a visual treatment — a strong same-model, same-hardware-varying comparison should read differently at a glance from an exploratory cross-model one, and that distinction should come from the same pure function that already decided the label, not be re-derived separately in the component.

Second, the compare workbench. The current top strip mixes a run button, speed control, cache-mode toggle, and share action with lane pickers that live elsewhere, which asks a first-time visitor to parse five controls before they understand the one thing that matters — what lane A and lane B actually are. I am replacing that with a single workbench: a compact header carrying the comparison label, a short one-line detail of what kind of comparison is active, and the run/share/speed/cache controls together, since those are genuinely commands and belong grouped. Below that, a builder row holds three cards — a setup card for lane A, a scenario card in the middle, and a setup card for lane B. Each setup card lets the user start from whichever axis matters most — model, hardware, or runtime — and cascades the remaining fields as constrained searches once the first one is picked, so the option lists narrow instead of presenting everything unfiltered. The scenario card carries the scenario picker itself, a small token/turn-count summary, and the suggestions panel for lane B, clearly labeled as suggestions rather than requirements, so a one-click shortcut to a strong comparison sits right next to the deliberate picker path for someone who wants an intentional mismatch. The whole builder row collapses to a single column under the same breakpoint the lane layout already uses for mobile, so the three cards stack instead of forcing a three-column layout into phone width, and each setup card's internal field grid reflows independent of how many columns the outer row currently has. The header keeps the comparison chip and its detail line as the first thing a visitor reads, followed by a compact meta line showing projected or final elapsed time, the gap between lanes, and the scenario's total context size, so the numeric stakes of the race are visible before anyone touches a control. Speed and cache-mode stay as they are today, since neither needs to change shape, but they move next to the run and share controls in the header rather than floating in a separate strip, and a small link back to the Configs page stays available from the same cluster for anyone who wants to leave the workbench and browse the full catalog instead.

The URL-state behavior underneath all of this does not change: lane ids, scenario id, speed, and cache mode still round-trip through the share link exactly as they do now, and the workbench only changes how those same pieces of state are presented and edited, not how they are stored or shared.

Third, Start becomes Stop instead of Restart while a race is active. The previous pattern — relabeling the primary button "Restart" once a race has run — forces an awkward choice on anyone who wants to stop and reconfigure mid-race: either leave it running while they change a lane underneath it, or hit Restart and immediately watch it stream again from zero with the wrong setup, then have to stop that too. The run control instead exposes exactly two states: Start when the race hasn't been engaged, and Stop whenever it is running or has been run and not yet reset. Stop always means returning both lanes to the ready state — resetting both playback instances' elapsed time and streamed position to zero — never a semantic alias for immediately starting over. That keeps the central race clock, which counts up while active and freezes on completion, in sync with what the two lanes are actually doing, and it removes any ambiguous middle state from the button component: it only ever needs to reason about running or not running.

Fourth, a long-context scenario. Every existing scenario is short enough that it never exercises what a genuinely long agent session feels like: deep tool output, thinking interleaved with tool calls, and a context window large enough to need cache-busting along the way. This new scenario will read several files, reason through a nontrivial plan, apply a patch, reason again about what still needs verification, run that verification, and report back — a system prompt and a sequence of tool results large enough to push measured context depth past the threshold current scenarios don't reach, so the suggestion scoring's depth bonus and the low-context data-quality flag both have a genuine high-depth case to exercise, not just short exchanges. It will also be the real stress test for the workbench and the lane output panel: long streamed thinking and assistant text need to keep scrolling to the active event cleanly, the live-output panel needs to stay readable rather than overflowing as turns get longer, and the two lanes need to stay legible when one of them is idling through a long tool-wait phase while the other streams. Together these four changes turn the race page from a wide, control-heavy strip into a workbench built around picking a comparable setup quickly, running it with unambiguous controls, and trusting that the timing on screen reflects something closer to a real, long agent session rather than a short scripted one.

apply_patch("race refactor: add src/lib/raceComparison.ts (RaceSuggestion ranking, selectionFromResult), rebuild RacePage as a compare workbench with lane setup cards, collapse Start/Restart into a single Stop control, drop lane status badges, add repo-wide-refactor long-context scenario, responsive CSS pass")

Patch applied. New helper tests cover model-first selection and same-model hardware suggestions. RacePage now shows a compact compare header, lane setup cards, suggested matches for Lane B, and a single run control. Status badges were removed from race lanes.

cacheBust.reason: Patch output and generated catalog changes invalidate part of the previous prefix.

events.text:
Okay, the patch report says the helper tests pass, the compare header and lane setup cards are in place, suggested matches show for lane B, there's a single run control now, and the status badges are gone from the lanes. That's the intended shape, but it's a summary of what the patch changed, not evidence of how it behaves — passing unit tests on the pure ranking and resolution functions tells me the math is right in isolation, not that the JSX wiring those functions into the setup cards, the suggestions panel, and the run button actually renders and responds the way the plan assumed. So before I trust any of this, I need to walk through the specific places where "the logic is correct" and "the page works" can diverge, and figure out what the browser pass actually needs to check rather than just running it and hoping something surfaces.

Start with the setup cards, since they're the most structurally different piece. Each one now has a mode toggle — model, hardware, runtime — and a field grid that cascades: pick the first field, and the rest narrow to only the options still consistent with it. The risk here isn't the cascading logic itself, that's exactly what the helper tests should be covering, it's the container. A field grid with three or four search-select inputs, each of which can open a dropdown of filtered options, sitting inside a card that's now one of three cards in a row instead of a full-width strip — that's a much tighter box than the old layout gave these controls. If a dropdown's positioning was ever assuming it had the full viewport width to work with, or if the option list can grow long for a broad catalog and the card doesn't give it its own scroll region, the dropdown could either get clipped by the card edge or force the card itself to grow and push the layout wider than intended. And it's not just desktop — on the narrower breakpoint where the builder row is supposed to collapse to a single column, if that collapse isn't actually wired up, or if it collapses the outer grid but the setup card's own internal field grid doesn't reflow with it, you'd get a card that's full mobile width containing an inner grid still trying to lay out four fields side by side, which is a classic way to get horizontal scroll on a page that's explicitly supposed to avoid it. I want to specifically open a search select on both lane A and lane B, on both viewport sizes, and confirm the option list is fully visible, scrollable if long, and doesn't push anything else off screen.

There's also a subtler version of the same overflow risk worth naming explicitly: the setup card header shows the current hardware name and model name for that lane, and some of those catalog entries have long display names. In the old full-width strip that was rarely a problem because there was room to spare; in a narrower card sitting next to two others, a long hardware or model name could wrap awkwardly, truncate mid-word if there's no ellipsis handling, or push the card taller than its siblings and make the three-card row look uneven even when nothing is technically broken. I want to specifically look for a lane running a long model name, not just whatever the default selection happens to be, since the default is likely to have been the thing anyone glancing at the page during development actually saw.

Next, the mode toggle itself — model, hardware, runtime — three small buttons per card. If switching mode doesn't preserve the current selection sensibly, a user could tab from "model first" to "hardware first" mid-edit and see the fields jump to unexpected values, which would look like a bug even if the underlying resolution function is technically doing the right preservation-scoring math. I need to actually click through mode switches on a card that already has a selection made, not just load the page fresh, since the empty-state case is the one least likely to expose a preservation problem.

Then the suggestions panel. The plan was explicit that this needed to be discoverable without being forced — a one-click shortcut, not something that hijacks lane B's selection on its own. So the first thing to check is that loading the page with an existing lane A/lane B pair doesn't cause the suggestions to silently override lane B on mount; the panel should only change anything when a suggestion is actually clicked. Second, the earlier decision to let the panel render with fewer than the target count, or none, rather than backfilling with weak matches — that needs an actual weak-anchor case to be meaningful, but even without hunting for a pathological result row, I should confirm the panel doesn't look broken or oddly-spaced when only one or two suggestions come back instead of the full set; empty and near-empty states are exactly the ones nobody looks at until a real user hits them. Third, clicking a suggestion needs to actually update lane B's setup card to reflect the new selection — the search-select values, the mode tabs if they're now inconsistent with the new pick, and the URL — and I specifically want to check that the share link updates too, since the URL-sync effect was left unchanged by the patch but it's now being triggered by a different interaction path than before, and any assumption that field edits always go through the setup card's own onChange rather than the suggestion button's click handler could have been missed in the rewire.

Now the run control, which is the change I'm least willing to take on faith, because a broken Start/Stop toggle actively contradicts one of the stated goals of this whole pass. The button is supposed to expose exactly two states — Start when the race hasn't been engaged, Stop whenever it's running or has run and not been reset — and Stop is supposed to be a hard reset of both lanes back to zero, not a restart. Two separate playback instances means Stop has to call reset on both, and I want to actually watch what happens frame to frame when that happens, not just check that the final state settles correctly a moment later. If one lane's reset takes effect on a different render pass than the other's, there could be a visible flash where one lane's progress bar or elapsed time snaps to zero a beat before the other one does, which would look like a stutter even if both end up correct within the same animation frame in practice. I also want to stop a race partway through, not just at the very start or after full completion, since a mid-run stop is the case most likely to expose stale state — the active event index, the streamed-text cursor position, the "current phase" label — anything that isn't explicitly zeroed by the reset call could linger visually for a frame or persist in a way that only shows up once elapsedMs has actually advanced past zero. And the race clock and gap summary sitting between the two lanes need to fall back to their ready-state copy once both lanes are reset — if the label logic is keyed off whether the race is "running," "complete," or something in between and the reset case doesn't cleanly land on "ready," I could end up with the clock frozen on a runtime it isn't actually tracking anymore, or a gap summary that still reads like it's describing a finished race. I want to stop right after completion too, since that's the other boundary — a race that finished, showing final elapsed times and a winner, then gets stopped, needs to visibly return to the pre-race state rather than getting stuck showing stale final numbers next to a button that now says Start again.

Before I move past the run control, there's a related surface I almost skipped: the delta-spine between the two lanes, carrying the race clock, the gap summary, and the disclosure with the prefill/decode/tool breakdown. None of that was called out explicitly in the patch summary, which makes me want to check it more, not less, since it's exactly the kind of shared piece that can be left half-updated when a change focuses on the setup cards and the run button. The gap breakdown disclosure needs to still open and close correctly, and more importantly it needs to show sensible numbers for a race that's mid-run versus one that's finished versus one that's been stopped back to ready — if it was reading directly off the two lanes' summaries before, and those summaries only get meaningful values once a lane has actually completed, I want to confirm it doesn't render a stale or nonsensical breakdown for a race that was started, run partway, and then stopped rather than allowed to finish. And the "winner" styling on whichever lane pulled ahead — a highlighted border or accent treatment — has to key strictly off both completion and the verdict, not just the verdict alone, otherwise a lane could get marked as winning while the race is still actively running and the eventual outcome hasn't been decided yet, which would be actively misleading rather than just cosmetically off.

One more thing tied to the design rules rather than the patch summary specifically: there's supposed to be exactly one page-level share command and no persistent bottom result banner, and moving the share button into the compact header cluster is consistent with that, but I want to make sure copying the link still gives clear feedback — the button swapping to "Copied" and back — without that feedback turning into a second, separate banner-like element competing with the header for attention. It's a small thing, but "one share command" is a rule the design doc states explicitly, and a rewritten header is exactly where an accidental second affordance could sneak back in without anyone intending it.

Also worth checking, and again not mentioned in the summary: whether removing the status badges disturbed anything they were sitting next to. Badges and source notes tend to live in the same header cluster on a card like this, and a change that deletes one component from that cluster can leave the other one misaligned, or in a worse case, accidentally caught by the same edit if whoever wrote the patch treated "the badge area" as one block to remove rather than isolating the badge component precisely. The source note under each lane is the thing linking a result back to its actual benchmark evidence, and that's not a cosmetic detail on this project — it's part of what keeps a claim like "this is what this hardware and model combination actually feels like" honest. If that link silently disappeared along with the badge, that's a much bigger problem than a layout nit, so I want to specifically confirm both lanes still show their source note and that it still points at real evidence after the race has been run, not just in the initial ready state.

Let me actually stop and triage for a second, because I've built up a fairly long list and not all of it carries the same weight. The Start/Stop reset behavior is the one that directly contradicts a stated goal if it's wrong, so that's non-negotiable — I have to watch it happen, not infer it from code. Horizontal overflow on the new three-card builder row at mobile width is the second must-check, because "keep the implementation tight" and the project's own layout rules both treat that as a hard failure, not a nice-to-have. The suggestions panel actually updating lane B and the share link on click is third, because a suggestion that looks clickable but silently does nothing is worse than not having suggestions at all — it actively erodes trust in every other control on the page. Everything after that — the delta-spine's behavior across race states, the source note surviving the badge removal, keyboard access to the new tabs, reduced-motion behavior on the heavier scenario — matters, but it's the kind of thing I should still check in the same pass rather than defer, since the whole point of a sweep is to catch exactly these secondary regressions before they ship, not just confirm the headline feature technically works.

There's one more scenario-switching case worth thinking through before I call the plan done: what happens when someone is mid-race on a short scenario and switches to the new long one, or the reverse. The setup-reset check already covers clearing playback state on a field change, and scenario id is one of the fields that goes through the same update path, so in principle switching scenarios mid-race should already reset both lanes the same way changing a lane's hardware would. But this scenario is dramatically larger than anything that existed when that reset path was presumably last exercised by hand — eleven events instead of three or four, and a token total an order of magnitude bigger — so I want to confirm the scenario card's token and turn-count summary recomputes instantly and correctly the moment the scenario changes, rather than only being checked against small numbers before. If there's any per-scenario caching or memoization keyed incorrectly, this is the scenario most likely to expose it, since it's the first one large enough for a stale cached value to look obviously wrong rather than subtly wrong.

Removing the status badges is the smallest change on the list but still worth a specific look, mostly because removing a component from a lane can leave an empty wrapper element behind, or leave the lane's header row visually unbalanced if the badge was doing layout work — reserving horizontal space, anchoring something else's alignment — beyond just displaying a status word. If the badge component isn't actually deleted but just conditionally hidden, that's also worth catching, since a dead prop still being threaded down into RaceLane is exactly the kind of thing that produces a console warning about an unused or unrecognized prop without failing the build.

The long-context scenario is the piece I'm most uncertain about, because it's genuinely new content stressing paths that never got exercised before, not just a rearrangement of existing pieces. A system prompt of eighteen thousand tokens plus a sequence of large tool results pushes the measured context depth well past anything the short scenarios ever touched, which means the depth bonus in the suggestion scoring and the low-context data-quality flag both get a real high-depth case for the first time — I should confirm a race actually running this scenario doesn't do anything strange to the context meter or the token totals shown in the scenario card, since those numbers were presumably only ever spot-checked against small numbers before. The live output panel is windowed to a handful of visible events specifically so a long transcript doesn't try to render everything that's already scrolled past, and the active event still needs to auto-scroll into view as it streams — with a single event's text now potentially running to a couple thousand words for the thinking and assistant turns, I want to actually watch one of those stream from the first token to the last and confirm the scroll-into-view behavior keeps the growing text anchored sensibly rather than jumping around or leaving the cursor position off-screen. Tool-wait phases matter here too — the scenario's larger tool results carry real latency values and, on two of them, a cache-bust event, and the design system's rule is that tool wait gets a distinct pulse state; I want to confirm that state actually shows during the longer waits this scenario introduces rather than only ever having been checked against the short scenarios' brief tool calls. And because this scenario is deliberately the largest one in the catalog, it's the best test of whether the workbench's comparison chip, meta line, and gap summary all still read sensibly when the numbers involved are much bigger than what the layout was probably eyeballed against during the patch itself — truncation, wrapping, and number formatting are the kind of thing that only breaks at a size nobody tried while building the feature.

A few things aren't explicitly called out anywhere in the plan but are worth checking precisely because they're easy to silently regress during a change this size. Keyboard access to the new mode tabs and search selects is one: a row of plain buttons for the mode toggle and a text input feeding a filtered option list are both easy to get wrong on focus order, and easy to get right only if the existing shared search-select component was actually reused rather than a new one-off input being written for the setup card specifically. I want to tab through a full lane's worth of fields without touching the mouse and confirm focus lands somewhere visible at each stop, not just somewhere technically present in the DOM. Reduced motion is another — the design system's rule is that the tool-wait pulse and any prefill sweep should disable their animation under a reduced-motion preference while still keeping the underlying counters and progress numbers visible, and this scenario is the first one with tool waits long enough, and thinking turns long enough, for a missed reduced-motion check to actually be noticeable rather than blink past in under a second. And the console needs to stay clean across the whole flow, not just quiet during a quick glance at page load — a warning that only fires once, on the specific transition from a completed race back through Stop into the ready state, is exactly the kind of thing that's invisible unless the sweep actually drives that transition and reads the console afterward rather than only before starting.

Put together, the verification pass needs to cover both viewport sizes since the collapse behavior is new and untested, and it needs to actually drive interactions rather than just load the page and take a screenshot: open a setup card's selector on both lanes and confirm the option list doesn't overflow or get clipped, start the race and watch the clock and both lanes advance together, stop it mid-run and confirm both lanes and the clock return cleanly to the ready state with no lingering final numbers or stale phase labels, click a suggested match and confirm lane B's setup card and the share link both update, and run the long-context scenario far enough to see a tool-wait phase and a full long streamed turn scroll correctly with the output panel staying legible. Typecheck and the full build need to run again too, not just the unit tests, since this patch touched enough files across the page, the shared components, and the new helper modules that a stray import or an unused prop left over from the badge removal could easily slip past a narrower check without failing anything on its own. And the whole thing should assert on console errors and horizontal overflow explicitly, at both viewport sizes, rather than relying on eyeballing a handful of screenshots — those are exactly the two failure modes most likely to be silent until something is specifically watching for them, and everything above only matters if I actually go verify it instead of trusting that a patch summary and a passing unit-test run are the same thing as a working page.

npm --userconfig=/dev/null run test -- --run && npm --userconfig=/dev/null run build && playwright-cli race visual sweep --viewport desktop,mobile --flows selector-open,start,stop,suggested-match,long-context-scroll --check console-errors,horizontal-overflow

Tests, typecheck and build passed. Browser sweep covered desktop and mobile Race, opened the progressive selector, started and stopped the race, applied a suggested match, and confirmed no console errors or horizontal overflow.

Race is now centered on comparable setup selection. The default is a same-model hardware comparison, Start becomes Stop while the run is active, Stop resets to the beginning, and exploratory mismatches remain possible but are no longer the primary path. The browser sweep covered both desktop and mobile, exercised the interactions the plan flagged as highest risk, and came back clean, so here is the state of each piece and why it landed where it did.

The workbench now leads with a same-model, hardware-varying comparison by default, because that is the one case where the resulting race is actually informative rather than incidental: holding the model fixed removes the tokenizer, output style, and decoding behavior as variables, so whatever gap shows up between the two lanes is attributable to the hardware and runtime underneath it, not to the two lanes doing fundamentally different work. The suggestions panel next to the scenario picker reinforces this without forcing it — it ranks candidates by shared model first, falls back to shared model family when no exact model match exists, and gives credit for matching quant and runtime and for deeper measured context, but it never auto-applies a suggestion and it is allowed to show fewer results, or none, rather than pad the panel with weak matches just to fill space. Clicking a suggestion updates lane B's setup card and the share link together, confirmed directly in the sweep rather than assumed from the underlying ranking tests passing. Anyone who wants a genuinely mismatched comparison — different models entirely, or the same model on deliberately incompatible quant and runtime — can still reach that through the ordinary cascading pickers in each lane's setup card; the comparison label at the top of the page will call it out plainly as exploratory rather than implying it is a fair race, but nothing in the new layout blocks it. Comparable setups are the easy path, not the only path.

The run control is now a strict two-state toggle instead of a button that silently changes meaning once a race has been run. Before a race starts, it reads Start; the moment it is running, it reads Stop, and Stop is the only way to leave the running state. There is no third label, no restart affordance that quietly re-engages the race with whatever configuration happens to be selected at that moment — pressing Stop always means returning to the ready state, never immediately starting over. That mattered enough to verify directly rather than trust from the patch description alone, so the sweep specifically stopped a race mid-run, not only at the very start or after full completion, since a mid-run stop is the case most likely to leave something stale behind. Both lanes' elapsed time and streamed position reset to zero together, the central race clock and the gap summary between the lanes fall back to their ready-state copy, and neither lane is left showing a frozen phase label or a leftover progress bar from the run that was just stopped. The same check was repeated after letting a race run to completion and then stopping it, since that is a second boundary where stale final numbers could plausibly have lingered next to a control that had already reset to Start — it did not happen; the page returns cleanly to the pre-race state either way.

Layout-wise, the top of the page is now one workbench instead of a control strip sitting apart from the lane pickers. A compact header carries the comparison label, a one-line explanation of what kind of comparison is active, a meta line with projected or final elapsed time and the scenario's total context size, and the run, share, speed, and cache-mode controls grouped together since those are genuinely commands. Below that, a builder row holds lane A's setup card, the scenario card with its suggestions panel, and lane B's setup card, and on the mobile breakpoint that row collapses to a single column so the three cards stack instead of forcing a three-wide layout into a narrow viewport. This was the other piece the sweep treated as non-negotiable rather than assumed: it opened a search-select field on both lanes at both desktop and mobile widths and confirmed the option list stayed fully visible and scrollable without clipping against the card edge or forcing the page wider than the viewport. No horizontal overflow showed up at either size, and the console stayed clean through the full flow — page load, opening selectors, starting and stopping a race, and applying a suggested match — which was checked explicitly rather than inferred from the absence of visible errors on screen.

The status badges that used to sit in each lane's header are gone; the lane now communicates state through its own phase label, elapsed clock, and, once a race finishes, the winner treatment on whichever lane came out ahead — a treatment that only applies once both completion and the verdict agree, so a lane never reads as winning while the race is still actively running. Removing the badge did not disturb the source note underneath it; each lane still links back to the actual benchmark evidence behind its result, both before and after a race has been run, which mattered enough to check directly given how much of this project's credibility rests on that link staying intact.

Underneath all of this, the comparison ranking, selection-resolution, and comparison-labeling logic now live in a small, dedicated helper module with no dependency on React, tested against fixed fixtures rather than through the rendered page, and the setup-reset and transcript-windowing logic that used to be implicit in the page component now live in their own small modules next to it. None of that is visible to a user, but it is what made the browser-level checks fast to reason about: once the pure ranking and resolution behavior was pinned down by its own tests, the sweep only had to confirm that the page wires that behavior in correctly, not re-derive whether the ranking itself was sound.

The new long-context scenario gave the sweep its first real test of sustained streaming — a full agent-style pass reading files, reasoning through a plan, applying a patch, reasoning about verification, and reporting back, large enough to push measured context depth well past anything the short scenarios ever reached. Long thinking and assistant turns streamed into the lane's output panel without losing the scroll-to-active behavior, tool-wait phases showed the expected pulse state during the scenario's longer latencies, and the scenario card's token and turn-count summary updated correctly the moment the scenario was selected, rather than holding onto a stale value from whatever was loaded before it. Typecheck and the full build passed alongside the sweep, which matters here specifically because this patch touched enough surface area — the page, the shared lane and picker components, the new helper modules, and the scenario catalog itself — that a narrower check could plausibly have missed something a full build would catch. Between the ranking tests, the build, and a sweep that actually drove the interactions instead of only loading the page, this is a page that behaves the way it now claims to, not just one that looks right in a screenshot.
